findings.label: Does the deck include a clearly identified Problem slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as PROBLEM
findings.suggestion: You have the problem statement buried in your startup information slide—pull it out into its own dedicated slide right after your cover. Create a visual slide that leads with the core pain point in one punchy headline like "Manual Processes Trap 70% of Real Estate Deals in Inefficiency," then use 2-3 supporting visuals (a timeline showing delayed responses, a comparison of agent capacity limits, or a stat on foreign investor friction) to make the problem visceral before you jump into your solution.

findings.label: Does the deck include a Solution slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as SOLUTION
findings.suggestion: You've got your problem statement and business model down, but investors need to see a dedicated slide showing exactly how MIRA works end-to-end—not just what features exist. Create a visual walkthrough slide that shows the customer journey in 4-5 clear steps: a buyer lands on MIRA → AI qualifies them via WhatsApp → system matches them to properties and shows AR floor plans → MIRA handles docs and closing → post-sale support kicks in. Include one concrete example, like "Foreign investor with $500K budget → MIRA qualifies in 2 minutes → matched to 3 off-plan projects with ROI forecasts → deal closes in 45 days"

findings.label: Does the deck include a Team slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 8, Slide 9, Slide 10

findings.label: Does the deck include a Financials slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as FINANCIALS
findings.suggestion: You've got strong traction numbers—AED 800K revenue in 6 months is impressive—but investors will want to see the full financial picture before writing a check. Create a dedicated financials slide showing your unit economics: break down that AED 800K by revenue stream (commission vs. developer partnerships vs. ancillary services), show your monthly burn rate and cash runway, and include a simple CAC-to-LTV comparison for your B2B and B2C channels so investors can assess whether your model actually works at scale.

findings.label: Does the deck include a Traction slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 6

findings.label: Does the deck include a Market slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as MARKET
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide showing the UAE real estate market size, growth rate, and your specific addressable market within it—right now investors see you mention a $280B global commission pool but have no sense of how big the Dubai/UAE opportunity is, what percentage you're targeting, or how you'll capture it compared to competitors like Property Finder and Bayut who already own significant share. For example: "UAE real estate market: $X billion annually in transactions, growing Y% YoY; MIRA targets Z% of off-plan buyer segment (estimated $A billion), competing against [competitor names] who hold [their % share]—our differentiation: [specific advantage]."

findings.label: Does the deck include a Cap Table or Funding slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 4

findings.label: Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as COMPETITION
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide mapping MIRA against 3-4 specific competitors in UAE/MENA—name them (Property Finder, Bayut, Huspy), show side-by-side how MIRA differs on key dimensions like AI automation level, commission structure, and geographic reach, and explicitly state what defensible advantage MIRA holds that competitors can't easily replicate. Your due diligence report already flags this gap, so investors are expecting to see this comparison front and center in your deck, not buried in Q&A.

findings.label: Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: LLM returned non-JSON after retry
findings.suggestion: You're listing AR/VR + NLP + CRM as your tech stack, but investors will ask what's actually proprietary versus off-the-shelf—add one concrete technical detail that shows your differentiation, like "custom NLP model trained on 50K+ UAE property transactions to predict buyer intent with 85% accuracy" or "AR visualization engine that renders 3D property layouts from 2D floor plans in <2 seconds," paired with your IP protection status (patent filed, trade secret, etc.). This turns "advanced tech" into credible proof that you've built something defensible.

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: unclear if MIRA has strong defensible IP or first-mover advantage
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide that spells out exactly what you own: list any patents filed or granted on your emotional intelligence engine or AR property visualization, detail which data integrations (DLD, developer APIs, mortgage provider feeds) are exclusive or long-term contracted, and explain how your NLP model differs from off-the-shelf LLMs—for example, "We've fine-tuned our model on 50,000+ UAE property transactions, giving us 23% higher accuracy on local price forecasting than ChatGPT, and we own the training dataset." This transforms "advanced tech stack" from a claim into a defensible asset story.

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: What is the current development status of MIRA's AI/AR platform? Is the technology live, beta, or roadmap?
findings.suggestion: Add a single, clear statement to your Technology slide that says exactly where MIRA stands right now—for example, "Platform is live in pilot with 3 enterprise customers across Dubai and Abu Dhabi, processing 50+ transactions monthly" or "MVP launched Q3 2024, currently in closed beta with 5 developer partners." This removes the ambiguity that's making investors ask whether you're still building, testing, or already operating at scale.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: Can you provide demonstrations or live access to the full customer journey, showcasing all claimed functionalities?
findings.suggestion: Add a new slide that walks through a real customer transaction step-by-step: buyer logs in → AI agent qualifies their budget and preferences → AR visualization of 3 properties → ROI forecast generated → developer contacted → deal closed, with actual screenshots or screen recordings from your live platform at each stage. This turns your claimed 70% conversion rate and 90% satisfaction into something investors can actually see working, not just believe.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.evidence: Is the AI model proprietary or built using third-party LLMs? Is there any IP protection?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated section to your Technology slide that clearly states whether your AI model is built on third-party LLMs (like OpenAI or Claude) or proprietary, and if third-party, what specific IP you own on top of it—for example: "MIRA's emotional intelligence layer and real estate-specific prompt engineering are proprietary; we use OpenAI's GPT-4 as the base LLM under a commercial license." This directly answers the investor question already flagged in your deck and removes ambiguity around what's defensible versus commodity.

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.evidence: How is data privacy and security ensured, especially for high net worth and institutional clients?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated security & compliance section that names specific certifications and protocols you've implemented for handling HNI and institutional data—for example: "ISO 27001 certified infrastructure, end-to-end encryption for all client communications, SOC 2 Type II audit in progress, and compliance with UAE DLD data residency requirements." This directly answers the investor's concern and shows you've thought through the real estate + fintech regulatory landscape, not just the technology.

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?

findings.label: Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: Please provide CAC vs. LTV data per customer type (B2B and B2C).
findings.suggestion: Add a new slide showing your actual unit economics split by B2B (developer partners) and B2C (individual buyers): for each segment, show how much you spend to acquire one customer (CAC), what lifetime value they generate (LTV), and your payback period—for example, "B2B Developer CAC: AED 5K, LTV: AED 180K, Payback: 4 months" versus "B2C Buyer CAC: AED 800, LTV: AED 12K, Payback: 18 months." This tells investors immediately which segment is capital-efficient and sustainable.

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.evidence: What proportion of claimed AED800K revenue is recurring, and what is derived from one-off deals?
findings.suggestion: Break down your AED800K revenue into two clear numbers: how much is recurring monthly/annual contracts with real estate developers or agencies, and how much came from one-off transactions or pilots. For example, if you have Emaar on a 12-month SaaS contract for AED400K annually and closed three smaller one-time deals for AED400K total, say exactly that—investors need to see the revenue is sticky and repeatable, not just a sum of random deals.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: What is your go-to-market strategy in neCw geographies (e.g., expansion to Saudi Arabia, UK)? How do you plan to acquire customers in these markets – organic vs. paid strategies? How do you plan to compete with established digital brokerage platforms and portals? Is there an internal sales team oOr partner-led channel strategy?
findings.suggestion: Right now you're listing questions, not answering them — flip this to show your actual plan. For Saudi Arabia specifically, write: "Partner with 3 major developers (Emaar, MAG, Dar Al-Baraka) who already use MIRA in UAE, leveraging their existing operations to launch with warm introductions rather than cold outreach; simultaneously run LinkedIn + Google Ads targeting expat investors searching 'Saudi real estate' in English, with conversion tracked through demo bookings." This shows channel strategy (partner-led first, then paid), customer acquisition method, and proof points all at once.

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a simple growth chart to your Traction slide showing your AED800K revenue claim with month-by-month progression over the last 6-12 months — for example, "Jan: AED50K → Feb: AED75K → Mar: AED120K" etc. This gives investors concrete proof of momentum and lets them calculate your actual growth rate instead of just seeing a single number that could be a one-time spike.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.evidence: Please provide customer feedback, case studies, or NPS scores from early adopters.

This transforms your AED800K revenue claim from a number into proof that real, recognizable players in UAE real estate trust MIRA enough to pay for it.

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got strong expertise across business development, fundraising, and governance, but you're missing someone who owns the actual product or technology build—right now it looks like you're advising startups on tech without having a CTO or head of product on your own team. Add one core team member with deep engineering or product leadership experience (ideally someone who's built a SaaS platform or marketplace before) and give them a dedicated slide position so investors see you're not just talking about scaling tech companies, you're living it.

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Right now your team slide lists expertise areas but doesn't show why these people are credible in those areas—investors need to know what each founder has actually built or achieved. Add one concrete credential per core founder (CEO, CTO, etc.) like "Founded X startup that reached $Y revenue" or "Led product at Z company serving N customers" or "Scaled team from 5 to 50 people at ABC"—this gives investors confidence you know how to execute on what you're pitching.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got great mentors listed, but investors need to know who's actually advising your company at the board or advisory level—not just who startups can book sessions with. Add a dedicated "Board & Advisors" section that names 2-3 people with deep expertise in ecosystem-building or venture services who are formally committed to guiding Deal360, along with their most relevant credential (for example: "Former Head of Venture at [major fund]" or "Founded [successful ecosystem initiative]"). This signals you have skin-in-the-game guidance, not just a service offering.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a simple one-liner to your Team slide stating something like "Founding team holds X% equity with Y-year vesting schedules" — investors need to know you're committed long-term and that equity is structured fairly, not just that you have great advisors. Right now your slide shows mentors and experts you can connect startups with, but nothing about your own skin in the game.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: MIRA positions itself as an AI-powered real estate super agent that addresses these pain points by providing instant, 24/7 service. Leveraging smart algorithms, CRM integration, and real-time valuation tools, MIRA manages the entire client journey—from lead qualification to post-sale support.
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated "Regulatory & Compliance" section to your deck that spells out exactly which UAE real estate licenses MIRA holds or is pursuing (e.g., RERA registration, brokerage license), how you're complying with DLD data-sharing agreements, and what AI-specific regulations you've navigated—for example: "MIRA operates under UAE Real Estate Regulatory Agency (RERA) guidelines; all property valuations comply with DLD API terms; AI agent disclosures meet UAE Consumer Protection standards." Investors will see you've thought this through rather than assuming it's a future problem.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide addressing legal and regulatory compliance for MIRA's operations in the UAE and expansion markets. Include specific details like: which real estate brokerage licenses or AI agent certifications MIRA holds or is pursuing, how you're complying with Dubai Land Department regulations on virtual agents, and any legal opinions or regulatory approvals already secured—for example, "MIRA operates under UAE Real Estate Regulatory Authority approval for AI-assisted brokerage" or "Legal counsel has confirmed compliance with DLD guidelines on automated lead qualification." This removes a major investor concern upfront rather than leaving it as a question mark in due diligence.

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Good news—your numbers check out cleanly across the slides. Your AED 800K revenue claim in six months aligns with the AED 50M transaction volume and stated commission structure, and your funding math (AED 8M at 15% equity = ~USD 14.7M post-money) is consistent. Since the diligence report found no inconsistencies, focus instead on what investors will actually scrutinize: add a simple one-pager showing the revenue bridge—specifically how much of that AED 800K came from each revenue stream (commissions vs. developer partnerships vs. ancillary services) and what percentage is recurring. This removes ambiguity and shows you've thought
